artifacts.content:
Mom,

Figured I’d write instead of trying to cram all this into a quick call between the kids’ bedtime and my ice pack rotations.

The new injury from Feb 26 is still bugging me. Dr. Laura Peterson at Tanana Valley Occupational Health thinks it’s mostly an acute lumbar strain plus right shoulder and wrist, but it feels like that same L4–L5 spot that went bad back in March 2021 when I helped Dad move those tool chests in his Palmer garage. Remember how I ended up at Fairbanks Memorial Hospital for those steroid injections? It’s that same deep ache, right in the lumbar area, and sometimes it zings a bit if I stand too long.

What worries me is how many times this same area has been messed with. I’ve also been seeing the VA chiropractor at the VA outpatient clinic in Fairbanks since June 2023 for the old lower back and right shoulder strain from my short Army Reserve stint. Between that and the 2021 herniation, it’s like my back never really gets a clean slate.

The shoulder isn’t great either. It’s the same rotator cuff I tore playing men’s rec hockey at the Carlson Center in January 2022. The orthopedist at Tanana Orthopedics—also Dr. Peterson, different clinic—warned me back then that heavy overhead work might eventually mean surgery. Now every time it twinges I picture being laid up and trying to chase Caleb and help Liv with piano while in a sling.

Day-to-day stuff is normal otherwise—Rachel’s juggling her shifts at the clinic, Olivia’s working on “Ode to Joy,” and Caleb is obsessed with his snowplow toys. I’m just trying to do my stretches, stay on top of the meds, and not overdo it when I shovel the front walk.

Love,
Eric

metadata.subject: Back/shoulder update and the usual chaos at home

artifacts.content:
Mark,

Just sitting here with an ice pack and figured I’d give you the longer version of what’s going on, since my texts earlier were pretty short.

When I hurt myself unloading that pallet at the Cushman Borough Administrative Center on 2/26, it wasn’t just a random tweak. My right wrist has been a weak link since that cyst removal Dr. Harris did at Alaska Hand & Wrist Center back in May 2019, after Liv accidentally slammed the minivan door on it. There’s still that little scar, and lately it’s been acting up with that same carpal tunnel-ish numbness I first noticed in October 2024 when Dad and I were doing those side electrical jobs on a cash basis in that new subdivision off Chena Pump Road. Too many evenings crimping wires after a full Borough shift probably didn’t help.

Add in the fact that lower back and right shoulder have been on the VA chiro’s radar since June 2023 from the old Reserve strain, and it feels like everything hit the same side this time. When that box slipped, I grabbed with my right hand out of reflex and felt the wrist and shoulder go at the same time.

Day-to-day, I’m still trying to keep up with the kids’ stuff—Caleb’s preschool three mornings a week, Olivia’s piano lessons, the usual runs to Pearl Creek and back. Rachel keeps reminding me not to be stubborn and lift Caleb when he runs at me. Hard habit to break.

Anyway, how are things on your end? How’s line work treating you down in Anchorage this winter?

– Eric

Hi Megan,

I wanted to touch base before our next session at the UAF Community Counseling Center. The last couple of weeks have been rough. On January 25, 2026, I was lifting and twisting with a box of disinfectant at the Pioneer Park maintenance building and felt that same sharp pull in my lower back we talked about last fall when I came in for stress and pain management. It set off the same cycle—pain spikes, then I don’t sleep, then I start leaning on a couple extra beers in the evening just to wind down.

Since then, things have kind of piled up. On January 17 at the Noel Wien Public Library mechanical room, I felt a sharp pain and an audible pop in my lower back lifting a 60–70 lb box of custodial supplies. It reminded me a lot of the 2019 snowmachine accident near Ester Dome that Dr. Kevin Marshall at Fairbanks Memorial has been treating as a chronic disc issue. I keep thinking I’m past that, and then something like this happens and I’m right back to worrying I’ll end up off work again.

On top of that, I’ve been trying to keep up with Olivia’s anxiety stuff at Fairbanks Pediatric Behavioral Health and Caleb’s appointments, plus Rachel’s AA meetings. Between shuttling kids, my modified duty at Public Works, and the pain, I feel constantly behind and guilty—like I’m failing at work and home at the same time.

For our next visit, I’d like to focus on concrete ways to handle the pain spikes without automatically reaching for beer, and maybe some strategies for the nights when my back is screaming and my brain won’t shut off. I’d also like help figuring out how to talk to Rachel about all of this without just dumping stress on her.

Thanks,
Eric

metadata.subject: Back flare-ups and stress before next session

artifacts.content:
Personal note – context around this winter’s injuries and health stuff

1. Feb 3, 2026 – Cushman loading dock
Around 9:45–10:00 a.m. I was trying to finish moving a pallet of custodial supplies off the Borough Administrative Center dock on Cushman so I could leave by 11:00 to pick up Caleb from his occupational therapy session at Alaska Children’s Therapy Center and get back in time for Rachel’s afternoon AA meeting. I grabbed a case of glass cleaner and stepped into a wet, slushy patch where snow had been tracked in. Right foot shot out, fell onto my hip and lower back. Same general lumbar area that’s been a problem since the 2019 snowmachine accident near Ester Dome Dr. Marshall at Fairbanks Memorial treated.

2. BP meds and work pace
I’ve skipped my blood pressure meds a couple of mornings because they make me lightheaded, and after the November 2024 write-up from Mike Reynolds for working too slowly I’ve been pushing harder. Need to talk to a doctor about that—almost went down again carrying two boxes at once on a wet, snow-packed spot on the Cushman dock.

3. Family stress
Mom texted from Palmer about Dad’s upcoming cardiac stress test and the $1,200 balance they still owe Mat-Su Regional. I was reading it between trips with boxes and stepped back into packed snow, twisted my knee and back trying not to go off the dock.

Reminder to self: stop mixing rushing, wet floors, and phone distractions.

Dad,

Just wanted to give you a clearer picture of what’s been going on with my back and shoulder, since Mom said you were worried.

Honestly, I think a lot of this winter’s mess ties back to stuff we’ve done together. My lower back was already tight from helping you rewire the breaker panel at your place in Palmer the weekend of Feb 1–2, 2026. Then a couple weeks later at the Borough custodial storage room I was favoring my left side moving 45–55 lb chemical boxes. When I lifted one from mid-thigh and twisted to set it on the cart behind me, I felt that sudden sharp pain in my lower back and a pull in my right shoulder.

Same story with the wrist—those side electrical jobs we did off Chena Pump Road in October 2024 on a cash basis were when the carpal tunnel feeling first started. Long evenings wiring after a full Borough shift probably weren’t the smartest.

This isn’t the first time I’ve overdone it for work either. On Feb 1 this year I pulled a full overtime shift at the Noel Wien Library replacing ceiling tiles after that minor electrical fire in the archives. By the time I got to the Juanita Helms Administration Center basement mechanical room a few days later, I was worn out, bent to drop a box onto a cart, and my lower back tightened so fast I jerked my right arm and bent my wrist back trying to keep the box off the wet floor.

I’m not blaming any of this on you—if anything, I learned the “push through it” approach from watching you work. I just need to be smarter so I can keep doing this job and still run around with Olivia and Caleb. Maybe next time you’ve got a panel to rewire, we plan it for a weekend when I’m not already beat from shifts.

Love,
Eric

metadata.subject: Why my back and wrist are so touchy lately

artifacts.content:
Event: Medical & therapy day – Eric
Date: Thursday, March 12, 2026
Time: 8:00 a.m. – 4:30 p.m.
Location: Fairbanks (multiple stops)

Details / running plan:

8:00–9:00 a.m. – VA outpatient clinic (Fairbanks)
Follow-up with VA chiropractor about chronic lower back and right shoulder strain from short Army Reserve stint (been going since June 2023). Bring notes about recent flare-up at Borough Administrative Center loading dock and mention history of L4–L5 disc issues (herniation treated with injections at Fairbanks Memorial in March 2021 after moving Dad’s tool chests in Palmer garage).

9:30–10:15 a.m. – Tanana Valley Occupational Health Clinic
Check-in with Dr. Laura K. Peterson re: Feb 26, 2026 work injury (acute lumbar strain, right shoulder strain, right wrist sprain from lifting/twisting with custodial supply boxes). Ask how this interacts with older rotator cuff tear from men’s rec hockey at the Carlson Center in Jan 2022 (Tanana Orthopedics Dr. Peterson said might eventually need surgery if I keep heavy overhead work).

10:45–11:15 a.m. – Quick coffee / stretch break
Somewhere near Airport Way; use time to do PT stretches Megan Larson set up and jot questions for doctors.

1:00–2:00 p.m. – UAF Community Counseling Center
Session with Megan Lewis – focus on stress/pain management, sleep problems, and cutting back on “extra” beers when back pain spikes.

3:00–4:30 p.m. – Errands
Grocery run (Fred Meyer), pick up kids’ prescriptions if needed, home by 4:30 so Rachel can make evening AA meeting.

Note: Arrange neighbor to grab Olivia from Pearl Creek and Caleb from preschool if any appointments run long.

To: fnsb_union_rep@email.com
Subject: Context around recent back/shoulder injury and workload

Hi,

I’m not filing anything formal right now, but I wanted to put some context in writing in case things snowball later.

On January 29, 2026, I was at the Carlson Center loading area rushing to unload about twelve 40–50 lb boxes of cleaning chemicals after a 10-hour shift with only one 20-minute break. My supervisor, Mike Reynolds, had informally warned me the week before that if I didn’t clear the backlog of supply deliveries by the end of the month, it could affect my next performance evaluation. While hurrying with the boxes, I felt acute low-back strain and pain in my right shoulder and wrist start up.

That wasn’t the first time pressure about pace has been tied to pain. I’m already on a 90-day performance improvement plan from Karen McAllister for not keeping up with work orders back in December. On January 22, 2026 at the Juanita Helms Administration Center storage room, I told my coworker Jason Miller I didn’t want to slow down because of that PIP, rushed the last three boxes in a cramped corner, twisted quickly at about waist height, and my lower back and right shoulder pulled so bad I couldn’t straighten up.

There’s also an older piece: on February 10, 2026, I had severe back spasms lifting mop bucket boxes at the Borough Main Shop. When I got home I ended up taking one leftover oxycodone tablet from a 2022 prescription Dr. Patel at Fairbanks Memorial gave me after minor hernia surgery. I know I’m supposed to avoid narcotics around work, which is part of why I’m nervous about how this is going.

I like my job and want to keep doing it safely. I just feel caught between medical limits and pressure to move faster.

Thanks,
Eric Johnson
Facilities Maintenance Technician

metadata.subject: Workload, prior warnings, and recent back/shoulder issues

artifacts.content:
From: Eric Michael Johnson <eric.johnson@fnsb.gov>
To: Rachel Johnson <rachel.a.johnson@email.com>
Date: February 6, 2026 7:42 PM
Subject: What actually happened with my back/wrist

Rach,

Figured I should write this out while it’s still fresh, since workers’ comp and HR are going to want details and my story keeps getting more complicated.

The main one was the morning of February 3, 2026 at the Borough Administrative Center loading dock on Cushman Street. I was trying to clear that pallet of custodial supplies before my 10:30 a.m. phone appointment with Dr. Karen Leland at Fairbanks Behavioral Health. Being on that last‑chance agreement from the August 2025 attendance write‑up tied to my depression treatment has been hanging over me, so I rushed. I was carrying a 35–40 lb. box of floor cleaner, stepped on a wet, snow‑packed patch near the edge of the dock, slipped, twisted to keep from dropping the box, and felt that sharp pain in my lower back and right shoulder as I went down.

On top of that, it’s the same right shoulder and wrist I aggravated lifting those paper towel cases in the Juanita Helms Administration Center loading dock. Allison Greene at Tanana Valley Clinic has been doing PT on it since October 2025, when I first messed it up in Palmer, AK breaking up that family argument. Remember when Dad shoved Mark into me and I caught myself weird on my right hand and shoulder? That prior non‑occupational shoulder and wrist injury never really settled, so this new fall and all the lifting/twisting since then just re‑lit everything.

I’m scared about how this all looks with my mental health stuff and that last‑chance agreement, but I’m trying to be straight about what actually happened.

Love,
Eric

metadata.subject: What actually happened with my back/wrist

artifacts.content:
From: Eric Michael Johnson <eric.johnson@fnsb.gov>
To: Mark Johnson <mark.d.johnson@email.com>
Date: January 22, 2026 9:18 PM
Subject: Back is messed up again

Hey Mark,

Long day. I jacked my back and shoulder again at the Juanita Helms Administration Center and I’m supposed to write everything down for the incident report, so I might as well tell you too.

This afternoon, January 22, 2026, Jason Miller and I were moving archive boxes from the second‑floor storage room down to the loading dock. By around 3:15 p.m. we’d already hauled about 25 boxes. I kept pushing because I’ve burned through most of my sick leave earlier this winter doing PT on that old L4–L5 disc issue at Foundation Health Partners. I’d even skipped my 1:30 p.m. stretch‑and‑core routine that my PT, Megan Larson at Tanana Valley Clinic, set up for the chronic back strain, since I had to finish the boxes before picking up Olivia and Caleb from Pearl Creek Elementary after‑school care by 5:30 p.m.

Around 3:10–3:15 I bent to grab one more 45–50 lb box from the bottom shelf in Aisle 3, twisted left to avoid the fire extinguisher cabinet at the end of the aisle, and felt a sharp, stabbing pain shoot through my lower back and up into my right shoulder blade. I dropped the box and grabbed the shelving unit to keep from falling.

All this on top of being wiped out from Caleb’s asthma ER run at Fairbanks Memorial Hospital the night of January 6, 2026. I only got about 3 hours of sleep before rushing into the Public Works main supply room around 7:30 a.m. the next morning and twisting with a 50‑pound box – that’s when this whole flare‑up really started.

Feels like my body’s cashing in every bad lift I’ve ever done.

– Eric

From: Eric Michael Johnson <eric.johnson@fnsb.gov>
To: Personal Notes <eric.m.johnson+notes@gmail.com>
Date: March 10, 2026 8:11 PM
Subject: Injury timeline for claim – DO NOT DELETE

Self,

Dumping this here so I don’t forget details for the workers’ comp paperwork, union meeting, and all the docs.

1) Chronic stuff: Dr. Patel at Fairbanks Orthopedic Clinic diagnosed the L4–L5 disc herniation back in March 2023. It’s been flaring on and off. I did PT at Foundation Health Partners and now with Allison and Megan at Tanana Valley Clinic. Dr. Lisa Morgan at Tanana Valley handles meds – including the 5 mg hydrocodone tablets she prescribed after the January 2026 flare‑up.

2) Feb 6, 2026: Around 9:15 a.m. in the Borough Administration Building loading area / Admin Center loading area, I twisted to keep a box from falling. My lower back was already flared from the L4–L5 issue, pain shot down my right leg and I almost dropped to my knees. I grabbed harder with my right hand, wrenched my wrist, and was rushing because Mike Reynolds had reminded me that morning I’m still on that October 2025 written warning for being late finishing a supply run.

3) Same period at the Facilities shop at the Juanita Helms Administration Center: around 10:30 a.m. incident where I’d taken one 5 mg hydrocodone tablet Dr. Morgan prescribed. I was a bit groggy; when the pain hit and I dropped the box toward the cart on the wet floor, I grabbed awkwardly with my right hand and bent the wrist back.

4) March 10, 2026: Hurt back/shoulder unloading the last few 45–55 pound boxes in the Facilities Maintenance supply corridor before my 10:00 a.m. meeting with supervisor James Miller about that written reminder from the week of March 3, 2026 for too many unscheduled breaks. Rushing, twisted at the waist instead of stepping.

Also note: earlier accident happened right after a 10:00 a.m. break call with Mom (Susan) about Dad Robert’s March 3, 2026 heart procedure at Mat‑Su Regional – I was distracted in the basement hallway by the boiler room at the Admin Center when I twisted and grabbed for the box.

– Eric

metadata.subject: Injury timeline for claim – DO NOT DELETE

artifacts.content:
From: eric.johnson@fnsb.gov
To: karen.lewis@akbehavioralhealth.org
Date: January 14, 2026, 8:47 a.m.
Subject: Context for this morning’s call & injury

Hi Dr. Lewis,

I wanted to give you a heads-up before our 9:00 a.m. phone appointment with you at the Alaska Behavioral Health clinic here in Fairbanks.

I hurt my back and shoulder at work about 30–40 minutes ago. I was in the Facilities Maintenance loading area at the Fairbanks North Star Borough building trying to finish unloading a pallet of custodial supplies before I had to step away for our call. I still had about 10–12 boxes left, each around 45–55 pounds, and I was rushing because I was thinking about what we’d be discussing about my anxiety and sleep issues and didn’t want to leave the pallet half-done.

Instead of slowing down and turning my whole body, I twisted at the waist with one of the 45–55 pound boxes to get it onto the cart. As soon as I did that, I felt a sharp pain in my lower back and up into my right shoulder and wrist. I can still walk, but it’s tight and pretty painful.

I’m going to let my supervisor know and probably head over to occupational health after our call, but I didn’t want to cancel last minute. This feels connected to the stress and the way I push myself, so it might actually be important to talk through. If you think we should reschedule once I know more, I’m open to that too.

Thanks,

Eric Michael Johnson
Facilities Maintenance Technician

metadata.subject: Context for this morning’s call & injury

artifacts.content:
From: eric.johnson@northstar-maint.com
To: rachel.a.johnson@example.com
Date: February 18, 2026, 5:22 p.m.
Subject: My back, work, and Olivia’s therapy

Hey Rach,

I’m home a little later because I had to fill out incident paperwork at the Borough. My lower back and right shoulder flared up bad this afternoon. I’ve been picking up those extra evening shifts cleaning at Aurora Christian School in Fairbanks since early January 2026 to help cover Olivia’s private speech therapy bills, and I think it finally caught up with me.

By the time I started unloading the pallet of cleaning supplies in the Borough’s main janitorial storage area today (February 18, 2026), my lower back was already tight from all the evening shifts. The boxes were the usual 45–55 pound boxes of cleaning stuff. I lifted another one from about mid‑thigh height and, instead of moving my feet, I twisted to put it on the cart behind me. Right then I felt a sudden sharp pain in my lower back and a pulling in my right shoulder.

They sent me over to Tanana Valley Occupational Health, and Dr. Peterson thinks it’s a strain, but with how it started she asked about the extra cleaning at Aurora Christian and Olivia’s therapy schedule. I’m worried about cutting back on hours when we’ve got those speech therapy bills, but I also don’t want this to turn into something long term.

Can we talk tonight about whether there’s any way to juggle the budget so I’m not doing so many evening shifts? I hate that this might affect Liv’s therapy, but I also need to be able to keep my main job.

Love you,
Eric

metadata.subject: My back, work, and Olivia’s therapy
